1:
"Then wrought Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man, in whom the LORD put wisdom and understanding to know how to work all manner of work for the service of the sanctuary, according to all that the LORD had commanded." The witnesses note Bezaleel, Aholiab, and the wise-hearted men beginning the work of the sanctuary as the LORD had commanded. Gill notes that when they began to work is not precisely said, but it is very probable they set about it directly, as soon as they were furnished with materials. The witnesses note Bezaleel, Aholiab, and every wise-hearted man, in whom the LORD had put wisdom, working all manner of work for the sanctuary according to all the LORD commanded; the beginning of the work by the Spirit-gifted men, all after the pattern — that the gifted workmen now set to the work: "Then wrought Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man, in whom the LORD put wisdom and understanding... according to all that the LORD had commanded"; the witnesses note the workmen, "Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man" — the chief and his helpers and all the ingenious artificers under them; the witnesses note the gift, "in whom the LORD put wisdom and understanding to know how to work" — that their skill was God's putting, the wisdom to do the work being his gift (31:3-6); the witnesses note the rule, "according to all that the LORD had commanded" — that all was done exactly after the pattern, nothing of man's devising; the witnesses note that God gives both the gift and the command, and that the gifted are to use their gifts in his service and by his rule; the witnesses note that the work of God's house is wrought by Spirit-given skill, after God's own pattern; so that the gifted men begin the work after the pattern; according to all that the LORD had commanded. The witnesses note Then wrought Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man, in whom the LORD put wisdom and understanding to know how to work all manner of work for the service of the sanctuary. The witnesses note according to all that the LORD had commanded.

The witnesses note Bezaleel, Aholiab, and the wise-hearted men, in whom the LORD had put wisdom, beginning the work of the sanctuary directly and doing all according to all the LORD commanded — the work wrought by Spirit-given skill, after God's own pattern; God giving both the gift and the command, the gifted to use their gifts in his service and by his rule.

So the witnesses make the verse the beginning of the work by the Spirit-gifted men, all after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the gifted workmen set to the work directly and did all as the LORD commanded, the work wrought by Spirit-given skill after God's pattern. The witnesses note the the workmen: "Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man" — Gill notes the artificers; the the workmen; the the artificers; the the chief; the his helpers; the under them. The expositors observe the the LORD put wisdom: "the LORD put wisdom" — his gift; the to work. The witnesses press the set about it directly: "set about it directly" — not delaying; the furnished. The expositors draw the lesson on the after the pattern: "according to all... commanded" — nothing of man; the after the pattern; the nothing of man; the exactly; the by his rule; the commanded. The expositors note the Spirit-given skill: wrought by Spirit-given skill after his pattern. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the according to all that the LORD had commanded, and the work. The verse thus records the beginning of the work — teaching that the gifted men wrought after the pattern, the work wrought by Spirit-given skill.
2:
"And Moses called Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man, in whose heart the LORD had put wisdom, even every one whose heart stirred him up to come unto the work to do it:" The witnesses note Moses calling the wise-hearted whose hearts stirred them up to the work. Gill notes it was not enough that they had qualifications for this work, but they must have a call to it from Moses, to whom the whole affair was committed, to see it done according to the pattern. The witnesses note Moses calling Bezaleel, Aholiab, and every wise-hearted man whose heart the LORD had stirred up to come to the work; the calling of the gifted, joining God's gift, the willing heart, and Moses' call — that gift, willingness, and call meet in the workman: "Moses called Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man, in whose heart the LORD had put wisdom, even every one whose heart stirred him up to come unto the work"; the witnesses note the call, "Moses called" — that "it was not enough that they had qualifications... but they must have a call to it from Moses," to whom the work was committed; the witnesses note the gift, "in whose heart the LORD had put wisdom" — the God-given skill; the witnesses note the willingness, "every one whose heart stirred him up to come unto the work" — that, besides the gift and the call, there was a willing heart, stirred up to the work; the witnesses note the lesson the commentators draw, that "all that are concerned in" God's work must have both the gift and the call, and a heart stirred to the work; the witnesses note that God fits, inclines, and calls those he employs; so that the gifted, willing, and called come to the work; even every one whose heart stirred him up to come unto the work to do it. The witnesses note And Moses called Bezaleel and Aholiab, and every wise hearted man, in whose heart the LORD had put wisdom. The witnesses note even every one whose heart stirred him up to come unto the work to do it.

The witnesses note Moses calling the wise-hearted whose hearts the LORD had stirred up — joining the three: God's gift, the willing heart, and Moses' call; all that are concerned in God's work must have both the gift and the call, and a heart stirred to the work, God fitting, inclining, and calling those he employs.

So the witnesses make the verse the calling of the gifted, joining gift, willingness, and call. The lesson the expositors draw is that those who served had the gift, the willing heart, and the call, all meeting, God fitting, inclining, and calling those he employs. The witnesses note the Moses called: "Moses called" — Gill notes a call needful; the Moses called; the a call needful; the not gift alone; the committed; the to him. The expositors observe the the LORD put wisdom: "the LORD had put wisdom" — the gift; the the LORD put wisdom; the the gift; the God-given; the the skill; the in their heart. The witnesses press the heart stirred: "whose heart stirred him up" — willing; the to the work. The expositors draw the lesson on the gift, will, call: gift, will, and call; the gift, will, call; the the gift; the the will; the the call; the meeting. The expositors note the fits and calls: God fits, inclines, and calls his servants. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the even every one whose heart stirred him up to come unto the work to do it, and the calling. The verse thus records the calling of the gifted — teaching that gift, willingness, and call met in those who served, God fitting and calling those he employs.
3:
"And they received of Moses all the offering, which the children of Israel had brought for the work of the service of the sanctuary, to make it withal. And they brought yet unto him free offerings every morning." The witnesses note the workmen receiving the offering from Moses, the people still bringing free offerings every morning. Gill notes Bezaleel and Aholiab received it of Moses, who delivered it to the several workmen as their work required, to some gold, to others silver, brass, fine linen, and the rest. The witnesses note the workmen receiving from Moses all the offering the people had brought for the sanctuary, and the people still bringing free offerings every morning; the distributing of the materials and the daily flow of the people's willing gifts — that the offerings come in freely, morning by morning: "they received of Moses all the offering... And they brought yet unto him free offerings every morning"; the witnesses note the receiving, "received of Moses all the offering" — that the chief workmen took the materials from Moses, who "delivered it to the several workmen as... their work required," gold, silver, brass, linen, and the rest, each to his task; the witnesses note the continuing gifts, "they brought yet unto him free offerings every morning" — that the people kept bringing their "free offerings," willingly, "every morning," a daily flow of liberality; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the people's hearts were so stirred that they gave freely and continually, the work wanting nothing; the witnesses note that willing-hearted liberality keeps coming, morning by morning, a beautiful zeal for God's house; the witnesses note that God's work prospers where his people give freely; so that the materials are distributed and the gifts keep coming; And they brought yet unto him free offerings every morning. The witnesses note they received of Moses all the offering, which the children of Israel had brought for the work of the service of the sanctuary, to make it withal. The witnesses note And they brought yet unto him free offerings every morning.

The witnesses note the workmen receiving the materials from Moses, each to his task, and the people still bringing free offerings every morning — the people's hearts so stirred that they gave freely and continually, the work wanting nothing; willing-hearted liberality keeping coming morning by morning, a beautiful zeal for God's house.

So the witnesses make the verse the distributing of the materials and the daily flow of the people's gifts. The lesson the expositors draw is that the materials were given out to the workmen and the people kept bringing free offerings every morning, willing liberality flowing continually. The witnesses note the received the offering: "received of Moses all the offering" — Gill notes delivered to the workmen; the received the offering; the delivered; the to the workmen; the each his task; the gold and silver. The expositors observe the as their work required: "as their work required" — each to his task; the as their work required; the each; the his task; the gold; the linen. The witnesses press the free offerings: "free offerings every morning" — willing; the free offerings; the willing; the every morning; the daily; the the people. The expositors draw the lesson on the hearts stirred: their hearts so stirred; the hearts stirred; the their hearts; the so stirred; the gave freely; the continually. The expositors note the zeal for the house: a zeal for God's house. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And they brought yet unto him free offerings every morning, and the gifts. The verse thus records the daily flow of the people's gifts — teaching that the materials were distributed and the people kept bringing free offerings, willing liberality flowing continually.
4:
"And all the wise men, that wrought all the work of the sanctuary, came every man from his work which they made;" The witnesses note all the wise men coming from their several works to Moses. Gill notes the ingenious artificers who were employed, some in one thing and some in another, either on the tabernacle itself or the vessels of it and things appertaining to it. The witnesses note all the wise men who wrought the work of the sanctuary coming, each from his own work, to Moses; the gathering of the workmen from their several tasks to speak with Moses — that the workmen leave their labour to bring word: "all the wise men, that wrought all the work of the sanctuary, came every man from his work which they made"; the witnesses note the workmen, "all the wise men, that wrought all the work" — "the ingenious artificers... employed, some in one thing, and some in another," each at his own part; the witnesses note the variety, that some wrought "on the tabernacle itself," others on "the vessels of it and things appertaining," every man at the task he was fittest for; the witnesses note the coming, "came every man from his work" — that they left their labour and came together to Moses, to tell him of the abundance of the offerings; the witnesses note the meaning, that the work was carried by many hands, each in his place, all parts going forward together; the witnesses note that God's work is done by the joint labour of many, each fitted and busy in his own part; the witnesses note that the variety of gifts and tasks serves the one work; so that the workmen come from their tasks to Moses; came every man from his work which they made. The witnesses note And all the wise men, that wrought all the work of the sanctuary. The witnesses note came every man from his work which they made.

The witnesses note all the wise men coming, each from his own work, to Moses — the ingenious artificers, some on the tabernacle, some on its vessels, every man at the task he was fittest for; God's work done by the joint labour of many, each fitted and busy in his own part, the variety of gifts and tasks serving the one work.

So the witnesses make the verse the gathering of the workmen from their several tasks. The lesson the expositors draw is that the work was carried by many hands, each in his place, all going forward together, the variety of gifts serving the one work. The witnesses note the the wise men: "all the wise men, that wrought" — Gill notes the artificers; the the wise men; the the artificers; the employed; the some one thing; the some another. The expositors observe the the tabernacle and vessels: some on the tabernacle, some the vessels; the the tabernacle and vessels; the the tabernacle; the the vessels; the appertaining; the each. The witnesses press the came from his work: "came every man from his work" — left their labour; the came from his work; the left their labour; the to Moses; the together; the to tell him. The expositors draw the lesson on the many hands: the work by many hands; the many hands; the by many; the hands; the each in his place; the together. The expositors note the variety serves one work: variety serving the one work. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the came every man from his work which they made, and the workmen. The verse thus records the gathering of the workmen — teaching that the work was carried by many hands, each in his place, the variety of gifts serving the one work.
5:
"And they spake unto Moses, saying, The people bring much more than enough for the service of the work, which the LORD commanded to make." The witnesses note the workmen telling Moses the people brought much more than enough for the work. Keil and Delitzsch note the workmen said unto Moses, The people bring much more than enough for the service of the work which the LORD commanded to make. The witnesses note the workmen, one in the name of the rest, telling Moses that the people brought much more than enough for the work; the rare report, that the people gave too much, a problem of overflowing willingness — that the offerings exceeded the need: "The people bring much more than enough for the service of the work, which the LORD commanded to make"; the witnesses note the speakers, that "one in the name of the rest" spoke for all the workmen; the witnesses note the report, "the people bring much more than enough" — that the gifts brought in were more than the work required, the people's liberality overflowing; the witnesses note the rarity, that this is a strange and happy complaint, that there was too much given for God's work, a thing seldom seen; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the people's hearts were so warmed and willing that their giving outran the need, a beautiful and rare zeal; the witnesses note that it is a blessed thing when the people's liberality must be restrained rather than urged; the witnesses note that a willing people will give beyond what is asked; so that the people give much more than enough; which the LORD commanded to make. The witnesses note And they spake unto Moses, saying, The people bring much more than enough for the service of the work. The witnesses note which the LORD commanded to make.

The witnesses note the workmen telling Moses that the people brought much more than enough for the work — a rare and happy report, that there was too much given for God's work; the people's hearts so warmed and willing that their giving outran the need, a beautiful zeal, a blessed thing when liberality must be restrained rather than urged.

So the witnesses make the verse the rare report that the people gave too much. The lesson the expositors draw is that the offerings exceeded the need, the people's willingness overflowing, a blessed thing when liberality must be restrained rather than urged. The witnesses note the much more than enough: "much more than enough" — Keil notes more than the work required; the much more than enough; the more than required; the the gifts; the overflowing; the the work. The expositors observe the one for the rest: "one in the name of the rest" — the speaker; the one for the rest; the the speaker; the for all; the the workmen; the spoke. The witnesses press the a strange complaint: a strange and happy complaint; the a strange complaint; the strange; the happy; the too much; the seldom seen. The expositors draw the lesson on the hearts warmed: their hearts so warmed; the hearts warmed; the their hearts; the warmed; the willing; the outran. The witnesses observe the liberality restrained: liberality restrained, not urged; the liberality restrained; the liberality; the restrained; the not urged; the blessed. The expositors note the give beyond asking: a willing people give beyond; the give beyond asking; the give; the beyond; the asking; the willing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And they spake unto Moses saying The people bring much more than enough for the service of the work, the which the LORD commanded to make, and the report. The verse thus records the rare report — teaching that the people gave too much, their willingness overflowing, liberality to be restrained rather than urged.
6:
"And Moses gave commandment, and they caused it to be proclaimed throughout the camp, saying, Let neither man nor woman make any more work for the offering of the sanctuary. So the people were restrained from bringing." The witnesses note Moses restraining the people from bringing any more offerings. Charles Ellicott notes Moses felt it necessary to interfere and forbid further offerings; by the expression "Let neither man nor woman make any more work," it would seem the superfluous offerings were of wrought work. The witnesses note Moses giving commandment, proclaimed through the camp, that none should bring any more for the sanctuary, so the people were restrained from bringing; the rare restraining of the people's liberality, their giving stopped because it was too much — that Moses must forbid further gifts: "Let neither man nor woman make any more work for the offering of the sanctuary. So the people were restrained from bringing"; the witnesses note the command, that Moses, finding it "necessary to interfere," forbade "further offerings," that none, "man nor woman," should bring or make any more; the witnesses note the proclamation, "caused it to be proclaimed throughout the camp" — that the order went out openly to all; the witnesses note the restraining, "the people were restrained from bringing" — that, strange to say, the people had to be held back from giving, not urged to it; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that this was a singular honour to the people's zeal, their liberality so great it must be checked; the witnesses note that it is a rare and blessed thing when God's people must be restrained from giving rather than pressed to it; the witnesses note that the willing heart needs the bridle more than the spur; so that the people are restrained from bringing more; So the people were restrained from bringing. The witnesses note Moses gave commandment... Let neither man nor woman make any more work for the offering of the sanctuary. The witnesses note So the people were restrained from bringing.

The witnesses note Moses forbidding further offerings, proclaimed through the camp, so that the people were restrained from bringing — a singular honour to the people's zeal, their liberality so great it must be checked; it is a rare and blessed thing when God's people must be restrained from giving rather than pressed to it, the willing heart needing the bridle more than the spur.

So the witnesses make the verse the rare restraining of the people's liberality. The lesson the expositors draw is that the people were held back from giving, their zeal so great it must be checked, a blessed thing when God's people need the bridle more than the spur. The witnesses note the gave commandment: "Moses gave commandment" — Ellicott notes felt it necessary; the gave commandment; the felt it necessary; the to interfere; the forbid; the further offerings. The expositors observe the proclaimed: "proclaimed throughout the camp" — openly; the proclaimed; the openly; the the order; the to all; the went out. The witnesses press the no more: "make any more work" — none to bring; the no more; the none to bring; the man nor woman; the forbidden; the the offering. The expositors draw the lesson on the restrained: "restrained from bringing" — held back; the restrained; the held back; the from giving; the not urged; the strange. The expositors note the bridle not spur: the willing heart needs the bridle not the spur. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the So the people were restrained from bringing, and the restraining. The verse thus records the restraining of the people — teaching that they were held back from giving, their zeal so great it must be checked, the willing heart needing the bridle more than the spur.
7:
"For the stuff they had was sufficient for all the work to make it, and too much." The witnesses note that the materials were sufficient for all the work, and more than enough. Gill notes the gold, silver, brass, precious stones, wood, and yarn already brought in were sufficient for everything they were ordered to make, and indeed too much. The witnesses note that the materials the people had brought were sufficient for all the work, and even too much; the abundant sufficiency of the offerings, the reason the people were restrained — that there was enough and to spare for God's house: "the stuff they had was sufficient for all the work to make it, and too much"; the witnesses note the sufficiency, "sufficient for all the work to make it" — that "the gold, silver, brass, precious stones... wood and yarn" brought in were "sufficient for everything they were ordered to make"; the witnesses note the overplus, "and too much" — that there was even more than was needed, an abundance beyond the work; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the willing liberality of the people had provided not only enough but a surplus, so that the giving had to be stopped; the witnesses note that where the heart is willing, God's work shall not want, but be supplied with abundance; the witnesses note the rare honour, that the offerings were not too little but too much; the witnesses note that a free and willing people will more than supply the needs of God's house; so that the materials are more than sufficient for the work; and too much. The witnesses note For the stuff they had was sufficient for all the work to make it. The witnesses note and too much.

The witnesses note the materials more than sufficient for all the work — gold, silver, brass, wood, and yarn enough for everything, and even too much; the willing liberality of the people providing not only enough but a surplus, so that the giving had to be stopped; where the heart is willing, God's work shall not want but be supplied with abundance.

So the witnesses make the verse the abundant sufficiency of the offerings. The lesson the expositors draw is that the materials were more than enough for the work, a surplus provided by willing liberality, God's work not wanting where the heart is willing. The witnesses note the sufficient: "sufficient for all the work" — Gill notes everything ordered; the sufficient; the everything ordered; the the gold; the the silver; the the wood. The expositors observe the too much: "and too much" — an overplus; the too much; the an overplus; the more than needed; the abundance; the beyond the work. The witnesses press the willing liberality: willing liberality provided it; the willing liberality; the willing; the liberality; the provided; the the surplus. The expositors draw the lesson on the not want: God's work shall not want; the not want; the God's work; the shall not; the want; the supplied. The witnesses observe the abundance: supplied with abundance; the abundance; the supplied; the abundance; the the work; the the house. The expositors note the not too little: not too little but too much; the not too little; the not too little; the but too much; the the offerings; the rare. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the For the stuff they had was sufficient for all the work to make it, the and too much, and the sufficiency. The verse thus records the abundant sufficiency — teaching that the materials were more than enough, the willing liberality of the people providing a surplus.
8:
"And every wise hearted man among them that wrought the work of the tabernacle made ten curtains of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet: with cherubims of cunning work made he them." The witnesses note the wise-hearted men making the ten linen curtains with cherubim. Gill notes every wise-hearted man did the part assigned him, what he was fittest for and most skilful in; particularly some made ten curtains, which were properly the tabernacle. The witnesses note the wise-hearted men making the ten curtains of fine twined linen and the three colours, with cherubim of cunning work; the executing of the inner covering, the ten linen curtains, after the pattern — that the curtains commanded are now made: "every wise hearted man... made ten curtains of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet: with cherubims of cunning work made he them"; the witnesses note the workmen, "every wise hearted man... did the part assigned him, what he was fittest for and most skilful in," each at his task; the witnesses note the curtains, "ten curtains of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet" — the rich inner curtains, "which were properly the tabernacle," made just as commanded (26:1); the witnesses note the figures, "with cherubims of cunning work" — the embroidered cherubim, as appointed; the witnesses note that the making answered exactly to the command, the work done after the pattern; the witnesses note the type, as before, that the tabernacle of fine linen and colours with cherubim figures Christ, God's dwelling among men, and the church; the witnesses note that what God commanded was now faithfully wrought; so that the ten linen curtains are made after the pattern; with cherubims of cunning work made he them. The witnesses note every wise hearted man among them that wrought the work of the tabernacle made ten curtains of fine twined linen, and blue, and purple, and scarlet. The witnesses note with cherubims of cunning work made he them.

The witnesses note the wise-hearted men making the ten linen curtains with cherubim, each at the task he was fittest for, just as commanded — the inner covering of the tabernacle wrought after the pattern; the tabernacle of fine linen and colours with cherubim figuring Christ, God's dwelling among men, and the church, what God commanded faithfully wrought.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the inner covering after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the ten linen curtains with cherubim were made just as commanded, each man at his task, the work answering exactly to the pattern. The witnesses note the every wise hearted: "every wise hearted man" — Gill notes his assigned part; the every wise hearted; the his assigned part; the fittest; the skilful; the each. The expositors observe the ten curtains: "ten curtains of fine twined linen" — the tabernacle; the ten curtains; the the tabernacle; the the colours; the the inner; the rich. The witnesses press the cherubims: "with cherubims of cunning work" — embroidered; the cherubims; the embroidered; the as appointed; the the figures; the cunning work. The expositors draw the lesson on the answered the command: the work answered the command; the answered the command; the answered; the the command; the the pattern; the exactly. The expositors note the faithfully wrought: what God commanded faithfully wrought. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the with cherubims of cunning work made he them, and the curtains. The verse thus records the making of the curtains — teaching that the ten linen curtains with cherubim were made just as commanded, after the pattern.
9:
"The length of one curtain was twenty and eight cubits, and the breadth of one curtain four cubits: the curtains were all of one size." The witnesses note the measure of each curtain, twenty-eight cubits by four, all of one size. Keil and Delitzsch note the length of one curtain was twenty and eight cubits, and the breadth four cubits, the curtains all of one size, then coupled five and five, with loops and golden taches, so it became one tabernacle. The witnesses note that each curtain was twenty-eight cubits long and four broad, all of one size, coupled by loops and golden taches into one tabernacle; the exact making of the curtains to the appointed measure, joined into one — that the work answered the command in every measure: "The length of one curtain was twenty and eight cubits... the curtains were all of one size"; the witnesses note the measure, "twenty and eight cubits... and... four cubits" — the very dimensions commanded (26:2), each curtain alike; the witnesses note the uniformity, "the curtains were all of one size" — all exactly alike, that they might join evenly; the witnesses note the joining, that, as the commentators relate, the five were coupled to five, with "loops of blue" and "fifty taches of gold," so that "it became one tabernacle" (vv.10-13); the witnesses note that the making followed the command exactly, measure for measure, nothing altered; the witnesses note the order and exactness of God's house, every part by precise rule after the pattern; the witnesses note the lesson, that the many curtains, joined by the golden taches, became one, a figure of the church made one; so that the curtains are made to measure and joined into one; the curtains were all of one size. The witnesses note The length of one curtain was twenty and eight cubits, and the breadth of one curtain four cubits. The witnesses note the curtains were all of one size.

The witnesses note each curtain made to the appointed measure, twenty-eight cubits by four, all of one size, and coupled by loops and golden taches into one tabernacle — the work answering the command exactly, measure for measure; the many curtains, joined by the golden taches, becoming one, a figure of the church made one.

So the witnesses make the verse the exact making of the curtains to measure, joined into one. The lesson the expositors draw is that the curtains were made all of one size, just as commanded, and joined by golden taches into one tabernacle, a figure of the church made one. The witnesses note the the measure: "twenty and eight cubits... four cubits" — Keil notes as commanded; the the measure; the as commanded; the the dimensions; the each curtain; the alike. The expositors observe the all of one size: "all of one size" — that they join evenly; the all of one size; the join evenly; the alike; the exactly; the uniform. The witnesses press the loops and taches: coupled by loops and golden taches; the loops and taches; the coupled; the loops; the taches; the golden. The expositors draw the lesson on the became one: "it became one tabernacle" — the many made one; the became one; the the many; the made one; the joined; the whole. The expositors note the the church one: a figure of the church made one. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the curtains were all of one size, and the measure. The verse thus records the making of the curtains to measure — teaching that they were made all of one size and joined into one tabernacle, a figure of the church made one.
14:
"And he made curtains of goats' hair for the tent over the tabernacle: eleven curtains he made them." The witnesses note the eleven goats'-hair curtains made for the tent over the tabernacle. Matthew Henry notes the shelter and special protection that the church is under are signified by the curtains of hair-cloth spread over the tabernacle, and the coverings of rams' skins and badgers' skins over them; God has provided for his people a shadow from the heat and a covert from storm and rain. The witnesses note the making of the eleven goats'-hair curtains for the tent over the tabernacle; the outer covering wrought after the pattern, the protection of God's house — that the coverings signify the church's shelter under God: "he made curtains of goats' hair for the tent over the tabernacle: eleven curtains he made them"; the witnesses note the making, "curtains of goats' hair... for the tent over the tabernacle" — the eleven curtains of goats' hair, made just as commanded (26:7), to cover the inner curtains; the witnesses note the great meaning Henry draws, that "the shelter and special protection that the church is under are signified by the curtains of hair-cloth... and the covering of rams' skins and badgers' skins," that God has "provided for his people a shadow from the heat, and a covert from storm and rain" (Isa. 4:6); the witnesses note that God's people are "armed against all weathers," protected from heat and tempest, and "from the storms of divine wrath"; the witnesses note that those who dwell in God's house find shelter, that, be the tempest ever so violent, "it does not rain in"; the witnesses note that the coverings of the tabernacle figure the safe shelter of the church under God's protection; so that the goats'-hair curtains cover the tabernacle, a figure of shelter; eleven curtains he made them. The witnesses note And he made curtains of goats' hair for the tent over the tabernacle. The witnesses note eleven curtains he made them.

The witnesses note the eleven goats'-hair curtains made for the tent over the tabernacle — the shelter and special protection of the church signified by the coverings spread over it; God providing his people a shadow from the heat and a covert from storm and rain, armed against all weathers and from the storms of divine wrath, those who dwell in his house finding that it does not rain in.

So the witnesses make the verse the outer covering wrought, the protection of God's house. The lesson the expositors draw is that the coverings signify the church's shelter under God, a shadow from the heat and a covert from storm, those who dwell in his house safe from all weathers. The witnesses note the goats' hair: "curtains of goats' hair... for the tent" — Henry notes the shelter; the goats' hair; the the shelter; the the tent; the as commanded; the the covering. The expositors observe the special protection: "special protection... of the church" — signified; the special protection; the the church; the signified; the the coverings; the over it. 19:
"And he made a covering for the tent of rams' skins dyed red, and a covering of badgers' skins above that." The witnesses note the two outer coverings made, of rams' skins dyed red and of badgers' skins. Charles Ellicott notes this verse corresponds exactly to Exodus 26:14, and relates the construction of the two outer coverings. The witnesses note the making of the two outermost coverings, of rams' skins dyed red and of badgers' skins above; the executing of the protective coverings after the pattern — that the rams' and badgers' skins are made as commanded: "he made a covering for the tent of rams' skins dyed red, and a covering of badgers' skins above that"; the witnesses note that this "corresponds exactly to Exodus 26:14," the command now carried out; the witnesses note the third covering, "rams' skins dyed red" — the skins of rams dyed red, set over the goats'-hair; the witnesses note the fourth and outermost, "badgers' skins above that" — the plain, weather-proof covering outermost; the witnesses note the type drawn before, that the rams' skins dyed red shadow the sufferings and atoning blood of Christ, and the plain badgers' skins his lowly outward appearance, who "hath no form nor comeliness" (Isa. 53:2), the glory hidden within; the witnesses note that the making answered the command exactly, the work done after the pattern; the witnesses note that all the coverings, inner and outer, were faithfully wrought; so that the rams' and badgers' skins are made as commanded; and a covering of badgers' skins above that. The witnesses note And he made a covering for the tent of rams' skins dyed red. The witnesses note and a covering of badgers' skins above that.

The witnesses note the two outermost coverings made — rams' skins dyed red and badgers' skins above — just as commanded (corresponding exactly to Exodus 26:14); the rams' skins dyed red shadowing the sufferings and atoning blood of Christ, the plain badgers' skins his lowly outward appearance ("no form nor comeliness"), the glory hidden within.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the protective coverings after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the rams' and badgers' skins were made just as commanded, the rams' skins shadowing Christ's atoning sufferings, the badgers' skins his lowly outward appearance. The witnesses note the rams' skins dyed red: "rams' skins dyed red" — Ellicott notes as 26:14; the rams' skins dyed red; the as 26:14; the the third; the over the goats'-hair; the the skins. 20:
"And he made boards for the tabernacle of shittim wood, standing up." The witnesses note the boards of shittim wood made for the tabernacle, standing up. Adam Clarke notes the boards, referring to the directions given before for their making (Exodus 26:15), the walls of the tabernacle of shittim wood, set upright. The witnesses note the making of the boards of shittim wood, standing up, for the tabernacle; the executing of the upright frame after the pattern, the strength of God's house — that the boards commanded are now made: "he made boards for the tabernacle of shittim wood, standing up"; the witnesses note the making, "boards... of shittim wood, standing up" — the boards of incorruptible acacia wood, set upright, as commanded (26:15), forming the walls of the tabernacle; the witnesses note that this answered exactly to the former direction, the command now carried out; the witnesses note the great meaning the commentators draw, as Henry observed on the boards and bars, that "the strength and stability of the church, though it is but a tabernacle, are signified by the boards"; the witnesses note the type before given, that the boards of incorruptible wood, overlaid with gold and standing upright, shadow the saints, made firm and incorruptible by grace, upright and standing in God's house; the witnesses note that what God commanded was now faithfully wrought; so that the boards are made, the upright frame of the house; of shittim wood, standing up. The witnesses note And he made boards for the tabernacle of shittim wood. The witnesses note standing up.

The witnesses note the boards of incorruptible shittim wood made and set upright, just as commanded — the strength and stability of the church, though but a tabernacle, signified by the boards; the boards shadowing the saints, made firm and incorruptible by grace, upright and standing in God's house, what God commanded faithfully wrought.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the upright frame after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the boards were made and set upright as commanded, the strength of the church signified by them, a type of the saints made firm by grace. The witnesses note the the boards: "boards... of shittim wood" — Clarke notes as 26:15; the the boards; the as 26:15; the incorruptible; the the walls; the upright. The expositors observe the standing up: "standing up" — set upright; the standing up; the set upright; the the frame; the the walls; the reared. The expositors draw the lesson on the the saints: a type of the saints; the the saints; the the saints; the firm; the incorruptible; the by grace. The witnesses observe the upright in the house: upright and standing in God's house; the upright in the house; the upright; the standing; the God's house; the firm. The expositors note the faithfully wrought: what God commanded wrought; the faithfully wrought; the faithfully; the wrought; the commanded; the the pattern. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And he made boards for the tabernacle of shittim wood, the standing up, and the boards. The verse thus records the making of the boards — teaching that they were made and set upright as commanded, the strength of the church signified by them.
26:
"And their forty sockets of silver; two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another board." The witnesses note the forty silver sockets made, two under each board. Gill notes the sockets of silver, the bases on which the boards stood, were properly the foundation of the tabernacle, two under each board for its two tenons. The witnesses note the making of the forty silver sockets, two under each board, for the boards of the second side; the executing of the silver foundation after the pattern, the boards standing fast — that the sockets commanded are now made: "their forty sockets of silver; two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another board"; the witnesses note the sockets, "forty sockets of silver" — the bases, "properly the foundation of the tabernacle," two for each board, made as commanded (26:21); the witnesses note the fitting, "two sockets under one board... and... another" — each board's two tenons set into two silver sockets, that it might stand firm; the witnesses note the type before given, that the silver sockets, made of the redemption-money, shadow Christ's redemption as the foundation on which the church stands and is established; the witnesses note that the boards rested wholly on silver, the whole house on the foundation of redemption; the witnesses note that the making answered the command exactly, the work done after the pattern; the witnesses note that what God commanded was faithfully wrought; so that the silver foundation is made, the boards standing fast; and two sockets under another board. The witnesses note And their forty sockets of silver. The witnesses note two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another board.

The witnesses note the forty silver sockets made, two under each board, just as commanded — the silver foundation on which the boards stood fast; the silver, the redemption-money, shadowing Christ's redemption as the foundation on which the church stands, the whole house resting on the foundation of redemption.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the silver foundation after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the forty silver sockets were made as commanded, the boards standing fast on them, a type of the church established on Christ's redemption. The witnesses note the forty sockets: "forty sockets of silver" — Gill notes the foundation; the forty sockets; the the foundation; the the bases; the two each; the as commanded. The expositors observe the two under each: "two sockets under one board" — for its tenons; the two under each; the two sockets; the its tenons; the firm; the stand fast. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christ's redemption: a type of Christ's redemption; the Christ's redemption; the Christ; the redemption; the the church; the established. The expositors note the answered the command: the work answered the command; the answered the command; the answered; the the command; the the pattern; the wrought. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And their forty sockets of silver, the two sockets under one board and two sockets under another board, and the foundation. 30:
"And there were eight boards; and their sockets were sixteen sockets of silver, under every board two sockets." The witnesses note the eight boards of the west end and their sixteen silver sockets. Charles Ellicott notes "under every board two sockets" is undoubtedly the true meaning, the eight boards of the back of the tabernacle resting each on two silver sockets. The witnesses note the eight boards and their sixteen silver sockets, two under every board, for the west end; the executing of the west wall and its foundation after the pattern — that the back is wholly framed and founded as commanded: "there were eight boards; and their sockets were sixteen sockets of silver, under every board two sockets"; the witnesses note the boards, "eight boards" — the six boards and the two corner boards of the west end together, made as commanded (26:25); the witnesses note the sockets, "sixteen sockets of silver, under every board two sockets" — two silver sockets under each of the eight boards, the back also founded on silver; the witnesses note that the west wall, like the rest, rested wholly on the silver foundation, the whole house on silver; the witnesses note the type before given, the silver foundation shadowing Christ's redemption, on which every part of the church is established; the witnesses note that the making answered the command, the work done after the pattern; the witnesses note that God's house is complete and founded on every side; so that the west wall is made and founded on silver; under every board two sockets. The witnesses note And there were eight boards; and their sockets were sixteen sockets of silver. The witnesses note under every board two sockets.

The witnesses note the eight boards of the west end and their sixteen silver sockets, two under every board, just as commanded — the back also founded on silver, the whole house resting on the silver foundation; the silver shadowing Christ's redemption, on which every part of the church is established, God's house complete and founded on every side.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the west wall and its foundation after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the eight boards and sixteen silver sockets were made as commanded, the back also founded on silver, a type of the church established on Christ's redemption. The witnesses note the eight boards: "eight boards" — Ellicott notes six and two corners; the eight boards; the six and two corners; the the west end; the together; the as commanded. The expositors observe the sixteen sockets: "sixteen sockets of silver" — two under each; the sixteen sockets; the two under each; the of silver; the founded; the the back. The expositors note the complete and founded: the house complete and founded; the complete and founded; the complete; the founded; the every side; the firm. The verse thus records the west wall and its foundation — teaching that the eight boards and sixteen silver sockets were made as commanded, the back also founded on silver.
31:
"And he made bars of shittim wood; five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle," The witnesses note the bars of shittim wood made, five for the boards of the one side. Adam Clarke notes the bars, referring to the directions given before (Exodus 26:26), the long poles that held the boards together and firm. The witnesses note the making of the bars of shittim wood, five for the boards of the one side; the executing of the bars after the pattern, that bound the boards into a firm wall — that the bars commanded are now made: "he made bars of shittim wood; five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle"; the witnesses note the bars, "bars of shittim wood... five for the boards of the one side" — the long poles of incorruptible wood, five to that wall, made as commanded (26:26), to bind the boards; the witnesses note that, as before, the bars "held the boards together and firm," joining the upright boards into one solid wall; the witnesses note the great meaning the commentators draw, as Henry observed, that the boards were "coupled together and joined by the bars which shot through them; for the union of the church, and the hearty agreement of those that are its stays and supporters, contribute abundantly to its strength and establishment"; the witnesses note that the bars figure the bond of love and the Spirit that binds the saints into one firm whole; the witnesses note that the making answered the command, the work done after the pattern; so that the bars are made, binding the boards firm; five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle. The witnesses note And he made bars of shittim wood. The witnesses note five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle.

The witnesses note the bars of shittim wood made, five to a wall, just as commanded — binding the upright boards into one firm wall; the boards coupled and joined by the bars figuring the union of the church and the hearty agreement of its supporters, which contribute to its strength, the bond of love and the Spirit binding the saints into one.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the bars after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the bars were made as commanded, binding the boards firm, a figure of the union of the church and the bond of love that binds the saints. The witnesses note the bars of shittim: "bars of shittim wood" — Clarke notes as 26:26; the bars of shittim; the as 26:26; the the poles; the incorruptible; the bind. The expositors note the bond of love: the bond of love and the Spirit binds. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle, and the bars. The verse thus records the making of the bars — teaching that they were made as commanded, binding the boards firm, a figure of the union of the church.
32:
"And five bars for the boards of the other side of the tabernacle, and five bars for the boards of the tabernacle for the sides westward." The witnesses note the bars made for the other side and for the west end. Charles Ellicott notes "for the sides westward," rather for the back of the tabernacle westward, the bars for the back wall as well as the second side. The witnesses note the making of five bars for the other long side and five for the west end of the tabernacle; the executing of the bars for every wall after the pattern, that all might be firm — that each wall is bound by its bars as commanded: "five bars for the boards of the other side... and five bars for the boards... for the sides westward"; the witnesses note the second side, "five bars for the boards of the other side" — that the north wall too was bound by five bars; the witnesses note the west end, "five bars for the boards... for the sides westward" — better "for the back" of the tabernacle, that the back wall too had its bars; the witnesses note that every wall, south, north, and west, was bound alike by five bars, all made firm by the same means, as commanded (26:27); the witnesses note that no wall was left unbound, the whole frame braced on every side; the witnesses note the meaning before given, that in God's house every part is alike bound and held firm, none left loose, the whole knit together; the witnesses note that the making answered the command exactly; so that each wall is bound by its bars, as commanded; for the sides westward. The witnesses note And five bars for the boards of the other side of the tabernacle. The witnesses note and five bars for the boards of the tabernacle for the sides westward.

The witnesses note five bars made for the north wall and five for the back westward, just as commanded — every wall bound alike, all made firm by the same means, no wall left unbound, the whole frame braced on every side; in God's house every part alike bound and held firm, none left loose, the whole knit together.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the bars for every wall after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the north and west walls too were bound by five bars, every wall alike made firm, none left loose, the whole knit together. The witnesses note the other side: "the boards of the other side" — Ellicott notes the north; the other side; the the north; the five bars; the bound; the like the rest. The expositors observe the sides westward: "for the sides westward" — the back; the sides westward; the the back; the the west end; the its bars; the bound. The witnesses press the every wall alike: every wall bound alike; the every wall alike; the every wall; the alike; the the same means; the as commanded. The expositors draw the lesson on the no wall unbound: no wall left unbound; the no wall unbound; the no wall; the unbound; the braced; the every side. The expositors note the the whole knit: the whole knit together, none loose. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and five bars for the boards of the tabernacle for the sides westward, and the bars. The verse thus records the bars for every wall — teaching that the north and west too were bound by five bars, every wall alike made firm.
35:
"And he made a veil of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen: with cherubims made he it of cunning work." The witnesses note the veil made of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen, with cherubim. Matthew Henry notes the veil made for a partition between the holy place and the most holy signified the darkness and distance of that dispensation, compared with the New Testament, which shows us the glory of God more clearly and invites us to draw near. The witnesses note the making of the veil of the rich colours and fine linen, with cherubim of cunning work; the executing of the veil after the pattern, that divided the holy from the most holy — that the veil signifies the darkness and distance of the old dispensation: "he made a veil of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen: with cherubims made he it of cunning work"; the witnesses note the making, "a veil of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen... with cherubims" — the rich veil, as commanded (26:31), to part the holy place from the most holy; the witnesses note the great meaning Henry and Wesley draw, that the veil "signified the darkness and distance of that dispensation, compared with the New Testament, which shows us the glory of God more clearly, and invites us to draw near to it"; the witnesses note the type before given, that the veil shadows the flesh of Christ (Heb. 10:20), rent at his death, opening the way to God; the witnesses note that the making answered the command exactly; so that the veil is made, signifying the darkness of the old dispensation; with cherubims made he it of cunning work. The witnesses note And he made a veil of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen. The witnesses note with cherubims made he it of cunning work.

The witnesses note the rich veil made with cherubim, dividing the holy from the most holy — signifying the darkness and distance of that dispensation, compared with the New Testament which shows God's glory more clearly and invites us to draw near, and the distance of our present state compared with heaven; the veil shadowing the flesh of Christ, rent at his death.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the veil after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the veil divided the holy from the most holy, signifying the darkness and distance of that dispensation, the way not yet clear, the veil shadowing the flesh of Christ. The witnesses note the the veil: "a veil of blue, and purple, and scarlet" — Henry notes a partition; the the veil; the a partition; the the holy; the the most holy; the rich. The expositors observe the cherubims: "with cherubims... of cunning work" — as commanded; the cherubims; the as commanded; the embroidered; the the figures; the cunning work. The witnesses press the darkness and distance: "the darkness and distance of that dispensation" — the old; the darkness and distance; the the old; the the dispensation; the not clear; the the veil. The expositors draw the lesson on the the New Testament: clearer in the New Testament; the the New Testament; the clearer; the the glory; the invites; the draw near. The expositors note the flesh of Christ: the veil shadows the flesh of Christ, rent. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the with cherubims made he it of cunning work, and the veil. The verse thus records the making of the veil — teaching that it signified the darkness and distance of that dispensation, shadowing the flesh of Christ.
37:
"And he made an hanging for the tabernacle door of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen, of needlework;" The witnesses note the hanging made for the tabernacle door of the rich colours and fine linen. Matthew Henry notes there was a veil made for the door of the tabernacle, at which the people assembled, though forbidden to enter; for, while we are in this present state, we must get as near to God as we can. The witnesses note the making of the hanging for the door of the tabernacle, of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen, of needlework; the executing of the entrance-screen after the pattern, the door of God's house — that the hanging is made for the door where the people assembled: "he made an hanging for the tabernacle door of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen, of needlework"; the witnesses note the making, "an hanging for the tabernacle door... of needlework" — the rich screen at the entrance, as commanded (26:36), of the same colours as the veil though of plainer "needlework" (without the cherubim); the witnesses note the meaning Henry draws, that "at this door the people assembled, though forbidden to enter; for, while we are in this present state, we must get as near to God as we can"; the witnesses note the type before given, that the door-hanging shadows Christ the door (John 10:9), by whom we enter into God's house and worship; the witnesses note that there is one appointed way in, and that the people are to draw as near to God as they may; the witnesses note that the making answered the command; so that the door-hanging is made, the entrance of God's house; of needlework. The witnesses note And he made an hanging for the tabernacle door of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen. The witnesses note of needlework.

The witnesses note the rich hanging made for the tabernacle door, of the same colours as the veil though of plainer needlework — the entrance where the people assembled, though forbidden to enter; while we are in this present state, we must get as near to God as we can; the door-hanging shadowing Christ the door, by whom we enter into God's house.

So the witnesses make the verse the executing of the entrance-screen after the pattern. The lesson the expositors draw is that the door-hanging was made as commanded, the entrance where the people assembled, a type of Christ the door, the people to draw as near to God as they may. The witnesses note the the door: "an hanging for the tabernacle door" — Henry notes where the people assembled; the the door; the where the people assembled; the the entrance; the the screen; the rich. The expositors observe the of needlework: "of needlework" — plainer; the of needlework; the plainer; the no cherubim; the the colours; the the same. The witnesses press the get as near: "get as near to God as we can" — draw near; the get as near; the draw near; the the people; the in this state; the assembled. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christ the door: a type of Christ the door; the Christ the door; the Christ; the the door; the we enter; the worship. The expositors note the answered the command: the making answered the command. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of needlework, and the door. The verse thus records the entrance-screen — teaching that the door-hanging was made as commanded, a type of Christ the door, the people to draw as near to God as they may.
38:
"And the five pillars of it with their hooks: and he overlaid their chapiters and their fillets with gold: but their five sockets were of brass." The witnesses note the five pillars of the door, their hooks and gold-overlaid tops, and their five brass sockets. Adam Clarke notes the five pillars with their hooks, their capitals overlaid with gold, made as before directed (Exodus 26:32, 37); there is scarcely anything in this chapter not touched on before, it being a record of the execution of what was commanded, to show that all was done according to the pattern. The witnesses note the making of the five pillars of the door, with their hooks, their tops and fillets overlaid with gold, on five sockets of brass; the executing of the supports of the door after the pattern, and the faithful record that all was done as commanded — that the door rests on golden-topped pillars set on brass: "the five pillars of it with their hooks: and he overlaid their chapiters and their fillets with gold: but their five sockets were of brass"; the witnesses note the pillars, "five pillars... with their hooks" — the five pillars of the entrance, with their golden hooks, made as commanded (26:37); the witnesses note the gold, "overlaid their chapiters and their fillets with gold" — the tops and bands gilded; the witnesses note the sockets, "their five sockets were of brass" — that these stood on brass, not silver like the veil's pillars, the brass befitting the outer entrance; the witnesses note that the faithful obedience of the workmen is recorded, that nothing was wanting; the witnesses note that God would have it known that all was done exactly as he commanded; so that the door's pillars are made, and all done after the pattern; but their five sockets were of brass. The witnesses note And the five pillars of it with their hooks: and he overlaid their chapiters and their fillets with gold. The witnesses note but their five sockets were of brass.

The witnesses note the five pillars of the door with golden hooks and gilded tops, set on five sockets of brass (befitting the outer entrance, not silver like the veil's) — the whole chapter recording the execution of what was commanded, that all was done according to the pattern shown to Moses; the faithful obedience of the workmen recorded, nothing wanting.

So the witnesses make the verse the supports of the door and the faithful record that all was done as commanded. The lesson the expositors draw is that the door rested on golden-topped pillars set on brass, and the chapter records that all was done according to the pattern, the workmen's faithful obedience set down. The witnesses note the five pillars: "five pillars... with their hooks" — Clarke notes as commanded; the five pillars; the as commanded; the the entrance; the the hooks; the golden. The expositors observe the overlaid with gold: "overlaid their chapiters... with gold" — the tops gilded; the overlaid with gold; the the tops; the gilded; the the fillets; the the bands. The witnesses press the sockets of brass: "their five sockets were of brass" — not silver; the sockets of brass; the not silver; the the outer entrance; the brass; the befitting. The expositors draw the lesson on the all done as commanded: all done as commanded; the all done as commanded; the all done; the as commanded; the the pattern; the recorded. The expositors note the faithful obedience: the workmen's faithful obedience, nothing wanting. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but their five sockets were of brass, and the supports. The verse thus records the supports of the door and the faithful record — teaching that all was done according to the pattern shown to Moses.
